title: Thinking Biblically About Life: Membership Matters
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: 11-3-24 - Thinking Biblically About Life 1 - Membership Matters - Pastor Tito Lyro.txt

outline.title: Series Introduction: Thinking Biblically About Life
subpoints: Seven topics: church membership, trauma and suffering, gentle parenting, adoption, life after abortion, infertility and miscarriage, morbid introspection
Pre-supposition 1: Biblical thinking starts with an unshakable commitment to the sufficiency of Scripture (2 Pet. 1:2-3; 2 Tim. 3:16-17)
Pre-supposition 2: What the Bible calls sin is always sin, even when the body makes obedience more difficult

outline.title: Is Church Membership Biblical?
subpoints: No single verse commands membership, but Scripture sets forth goals uniquely satisfied by it
Four ways Scripture addresses membership

outline.title: 1. Members Are Marked From Non-Members
subpoints: Old Testament: circumcision as the visible mark of the covenant people (Genesis 17:1-14)
New Testament: baptism replaces circumcision as the visible mark (Acts 2)
Physical marking distinguishes those inside the visible church from those outside

outline.title: 2. Members Can Be Brought In or Cast Out
subpoints: 1 Corinthians 5: Paul distinguishes those "inside" from those "outside" the church
Church discipline implies a defined membership roll
2 Corinthians 2: a "majority" vote implies a countable, defined body

outline.title: 3. Church Membership Is a Serious Matter
subpoints: Acts 5: Ananias and Sapphira; after their deaths, many "did not dare join them" (Acts 5:13)
The Greek word for "join" (kollao) means to glue or cement together — a formal, serious act
Those who were present but not joined were still considered outsiders

outline.title: 4. Membership Is a Commitment to the Entire Body
subpoints: 1 Corinthians 3: allegiance is to the church, not to a favorite minister
Church membership is a solemn commitment to every member, not just comfortable ones

outline.title: Church Membership Promotes Unity
subpoints: Membership identifies who we are to be united with
Image of a building/temple: bricks must be in the structure, not in a pile (1 Pet. 2:4-5)
Image of a body: every part is necessary; no Christian is non-essential (Rom. 12:4-5)
Gifts are distributed among the whole church — we all need one another (Rom. 12:3-8)
The Lord's Supper is a declaration of communion with one another (1 Cor. 10:13)

summary: This is the first lesson in a seven-part Sunday School series titled "Thinking Biblically About Life." Pastor Tito Lyro opens by laying two foundational pre-suppositions for the entire series: first, that Biblical thinking demands an unshakable commitment to the sufficiency of Scripture as established in 2 Peter 1:2-3 and 2 Timothy 3:16-17; and second, that what the Bible calls sin is always sin even when the body makes obedience more difficult. He then turns to the subject of church membership, arguing from four lines of scriptural evidence that formal, committed membership in a local church is a biblical concept: members are visibly marked by baptism and distinguished from non-members; individuals can be brought into or removed from the church through discipline, implying a defined body; membership is a serious act (described by the Greek word kollao, to glue or cement together); and membership is a commitment to the entire body, not to a favored pastor or subset of members. He concludes by showing how biblical church membership promotes church unity, drawing on the images of a building, a body, and the Lord's Supper.
transcript:
## Introduction: A Seven-Part Series on Life

Over the next seven times that I teach the Sunday School class, I want us to consider how to think biblically about several issues of life. Here are the seven things: trauma and suffering, parenting in light of "gentle parenting," adoption, life after abortion, infertility and miscarriage, morbid introspection, and church membership — which is where we will start today.

Before we dive in, I want to lay two pre-suppositions that apply to all seven topics. The first is that **Biblical thinking starts with an unshakable commitment to the sufficiency of the Scriptures.** In 2 Peter 1:2-3 the apostle writes: "His divine power has granted to us everything pertaining to life and godliness through the true knowledge of Him who called us by His own glory and excellence." Paul confirms in 2 Timothy 3:16-17 that "all Scripture is breathed out by God and profitable for doctrine, for reproof, for correction, for instruction in righteousness, that the man of God may be complete, thoroughly equipped for every good work." Nothing in life falls outside those four categories. We must seek wisdom from the Word of God.

The second pre-supposition is that **what the Bible calls sin is always sin, even when the body makes obedience more difficult.** We will explore this as each topic unfolds.

## Is There Any Biblical Indication That We Should Join a Local Church?

There is no verse that says, "Thou shalt become a church member." But the Bible does set forth a number of goals and requirements for Christians that are uniquely satisfied by church membership. Let us examine four ways the Scriptures speak about it.

## 1. Members Are Marked From Non-Members

In both the Old and New Testaments, God's visible people are marked by a physical sign. In Genesis 17:1-14, God establishes circumcision as the visible mark of the covenant people. The uncircumcised male "shall be cut off from his people; he has broken my covenant" (v. 14). In Acts 2, when Peter finished preaching at Pentecost, those who believed "were baptized and joined them." It was baptism — not merely belief — that made them visibly part of the church. Circumcision in the Old Testament and baptism in the New Testament function as visible marks for visible people.

## 2. Members Can Be Brought In or Cast Out

In 1 Corinthians 5:3-13, Paul distinguishes those who are "inside" the church from those who are "outside." He calls the church to remove the unrepentant man: "put away from yourselves the evil person." This change of status — being brought in or removed — assumes a defined membership. In 2 Corinthians 2, Paul references a decision made by "the majority" — which presupposes a defined, countable whole. The language of inside and outside in the New Testament demonstrates a clear understanding of formal commitment to a church.

## 3. Church Membership Is a Serious Matter

In Acts 5, after Ananias and Sapphira dropped dead for lying to God, verse 13 tells us: "None of the rest dared join them, but the people esteemed them highly." Many were present but were not part of them. The word translated "join" is the Greek word *kollao*, which means to glue or cement together, to unite firmly. Paul uses the same word in 1 Corinthians 6:16-17 when speaking of being "joined" to a harlot — an actual act of union, not merely physical proximity. Joining a church is a serious, formal act of gluing oneself to the body.

## 4. Membership Is a Commitment to the Entire Body

In 1 Corinthians 3:4-9, Paul rebukes the Corinthians for dividing themselves according to favorite ministers: "I am of Paul; I am of Apollos." He insists that membership is a solemn commitment to the entire local body, not just to those with whom we feel comfortable. The most discouraging thing a member can say is, "I am not sure I will be there because you are not going to be there." You are not a member of a particular pastor — you are a member of this local church and of one another.

## Church Membership Promotes Unity

Biblical church membership facilitates church unity by identifying the people we are to be united with and by requiring a commitment to the entire church.

Paul and Peter use the image of a building or temple for the visible church. Peter says in 1 Peter 2:4-5: "You also, as living stones, are being built up a spiritual house." The bricks must be in the structure — not in a pile — to be part of the building. Individual Christians must be woven into the tapestry of the local church.

The body image is even stronger. Paul says in Romans 12:4-5: "As we have many members in one body, but all the members do not have the same function, so we, being many, are one body in Christ, and individually members of one another." Every part of the body is necessary. None of us are non-essential. God has distributed gifts among the entire church so that every one of us needs the other (Rom. 12:3-8).

Even the Lord's Supper communicates our membership with one another: "For we, though many, are one bread and one body, for we all partake of that one bread" (1 Cor. 10:17). Membership matters. It is not a man-made concept to exercise power over people — it is a covenant commitment to one another to serve the Lord Jesus together, to love each other, and to stir each other up to good works.
